name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَوَلَمَّآ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُم مِّثْلَيْهَا قُلْتُمْ أَنَّىٰ هَـٰذَا ۖ قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٦٥)
```

```english
What, when an affliction visits you—while you have inflicted twice as much—do you say, ‘How is this?’! Say, ‘This is from your own souls.’ Indeed Allah has power over all things. (165)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٦٦)
```

```english
What befell you on the day when the two hosts met, was by Allah’s permission, so that He may ascertain the faithful, (166)
```

```arabic
وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ ۚ وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَوِ ٱدْفَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ نَعْلَمُ قِتَالًا لَّٱتَّبَعْنَـٰكُمْ ۗ هُمْ لِلْكُفْرِ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَقْرَبُ مِنْهُمْ لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَكْتُمُونَ (١٦٧)
```

```english
and ascertain the hypocrites. [When] they were told: ‘Come, fight in the way of Allah, or defend [yourselves], they said, ‘If we knew any fighting, we would have surely followed you.’ That day they were nearer to unfaith than to faith. They say with their mouths what is not in their hearts, and Allah knows well whatever they conceal. (167)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ وَقَعَدُوا۟ لَوْ أَطَاعُونَا مَا قُتِلُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ فَٱدْرَءُوا۟ عَنْ أَنفُسِكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٦٨)
```

```english
Those who said of their brethren, while they themselves sat back: ‘Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed.’ Say, ‘Then keep death off from yourselves, if you are truthful.’ (168)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتًۢا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ (١٦٩)
```

```english
Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord, (169)
```

```arabic
فَرِحِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١٧٠)
```

```english
exulting in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and rejoicing for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (170)
```

```arabic
۞ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٧١)
```

```english
They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace, and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful. (171)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses are a continuation of the verses revealed specifically regarding the Battle of Uhud. Within them is an address concerning the state of a number of hypocrites (المنافقين) who abandoned the group of believers (المؤمنين) when they went out from Medina to Uhud. They also contain a response to what they said regarding those who were killed, and a description of the state of the martyrs (المستشهدين) after their death—that they are favored in the Presence of Proximity (حضرة القرب), rejoicing in those of their brothers left behind.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمَّا أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها** — **"What! When a misfortune smote you, while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much..."** (3:165). When He forbade them from being like those who disbelieve in grieving for their slain and feeling regret over them—by explaining that the matter of life and death belongs to Allah alone and not to them, such that it would revolve around their proximity, distance, going out to fight, or staying behind—He returned a second time to explain its proximate cause (سببه القريب) according to the established custom of causes (سنة الأسباب). He explained that its cause was simply the disobedience (المعصية) that occurred from them on the day of Uhud; namely, the disobedience of the archers by abandoning their positions, and the disobedience of those among them who turned away from the fight thereafter. In short, the cause was their disobedience to the Messenger—who was their leader—their loss of courage, and their disputing the command. This is a cause for defeat according to the law of nature and custom.

Thus, the verse carries the meaning of saying: "Do you know from where this misfortune smote you, when you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much? It only smote you from your own selves (من عند أنفسكم); it was your own ruining of the cause of victory and triumph with your own hands, your opposition to your leader, your loss of courage, and the discord in your word."

The misfortune was described by His saying: **قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها** — **"while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much"** (3:165). This is an allusion to a comparison between what the disbelievers (الكفار) inflicted upon them on the day of Uhud—which was the killing of seventy of their men—and what they had inflicted upon the disbelievers on the day of Badr, which was twice that seventy. For on the day of Badr, they killed seventy of their men and took seventy as captives.

In this description, there is a calming of their agitated hearts and a belittling of the misfortune; for they were afflicted by their enemies with only half of what they had inflicted upon them. Therefore, it is not fitting for them to grieve or despair.

It has also been said that the meaning of the verse is: "You yourselves chose this misfortune." This is because they chose to take ransom (الفداء) from the captives on the day of Badr, while the judgment regarding them was execution. It was stipulated to them: "If you accept the ransom, an equal number of you will be killed in the coming year." They said: "We agree; for we shall take the ransom and benefit from it, and if any of us are killed later, we shall be martyrs (شهداء)."

This view is supported—indeed, indicated—by the conclusion of the verse, namely His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — **"Indeed Allah has power over all things"** (3:165). This phrase does not fit the previous view at all except through strained interpretation. The narration of this view from the Imams of the People of the House (أئمة أهل البيت) صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم will follow in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ** — **"What smote you on the day the two hosts met..."** (3:166) until the end of the two verses. The first verse supports what was previously mentioned: that the meaning of His saying: **قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِكُمْ** — **"Say, ‘It is from your own souls’"** (3:165) refers to their choosing the ransom from the captives of the day of Badr and their stipulating upon themselves before Allah what they stipulated. Thus, the occurrence of this misfortune was by the permission of Allah (بإذن الله). As for the first mentioned view—that the proximate cause of the misfortune was their disobedience—there is no apparent harmony between it and the attribution of the misfortune to the permission of Allah, as is evident.

According to what we have mentioned, the mention of attributing the misfortune to the permission of Allah serves as an explanation for His saying: **هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِكُمْ** — **"It is from your own souls"** (3:165), and as a prelude to the addition of His saying: **وَلِيَعْلَمَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"and that He may ascertain the faithful"** (3:166). By this addition, the way is paved to address the state of the hypocrites, what they spoke, the response to it, and the clarification of the reality of this death, which is being killed in the way of Allah.

His saying: **أو ادفعوا** — **"or defend"** (3:167) means: "If you do not fight in the way of Allah, then defend your sanctuary and yourselves." His saying: **هم للكفر يومئذ أقرب منهم للإيمان** — **"That day they were nearer to unfaith than to faith"** (3:167); the preposition (اللام) here carries the meaning of "toward" (إلى). This was their state in relation to explicit disbelief (الكفر الصريح); as for hypocrisy (النفاق), they had already fallen into it by that action of theirs.

His saying: **يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ ما لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ** — **"saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts"** (3:167). The "mouths" (الأفواه) are mentioned for emphasis and to create a contrast between them and the "hearts" (القلوب).

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ قالُوا لِإِخْوانِهِمْ وَقَعَدُوا لَوْ أَطاعُونا ما قُتِلُوا** — **"Those who said of their brethren, while they themselves sat back: ‘Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed’"** (3:168). What is meant by "their brethren" (إخوانهم) is their brothers in lineage, namely the slain. Their brotherhood was mentioned so that, when joined with His saying: **وَقَعَدُوا** — **"while they themselves sat back"**, it would be a more effective reproach and censure against them; for they sat back from aiding their brothers until they were afflicted by such terrible slaughter. His saying: **قل فادرءوا** — **"Say, ‘Then ward off...’"** (3:168) is a response to that statement of theirs. Warding off (الدرء) means to repel (الدفع).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً** — **"Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead"** (3:169). In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. The reason for this is what has been repeatedly mentioned throughout these verses. It is also possible that the address is a continuation of the address in His saying: **قُلْ فَادْرَؤُا عَنْ أَنْفُسِكُمُ الْمَوْتَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — **"Say, ‘Then ward off death from yourselves, if you are truthful’"** (3:168).

What is meant by death (الموت) here is the cessation of consciousness (الشعور) and action (الفعل). For this reason, He mentioned both in His saying: **بَلْ أَحْياءٌ** — **"no, they are alive"** (3:169) etc., where He mentioned "receiving provision" (الارتزاق), which is an action, and "rejoicing" (الفرح) and "being glad" (الاستبشار), which are accompanied by consciousness.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَرِحِينَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ** — **"rejoicing in what Allah has given them..."** (3:170). Joy (الفرح) is the opposite of grief (الحزن). Good tidings (البشارة) or (البشرى) is news that gladdens you, and being glad (الاستبشار) is the seeking of joy through good tidings. The meaning is: they are joyful because of the divine grace (الفضل الإلهي) they have found present and witnessed with them, and they seek joy through the good tidings that reach them regarding the good state of those who have not yet joined them from behind—that no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve.

From this, several points become clear: First, that news of the best of the believers remaining after them in the world reaches and connects with those killed in the way of Allah.

Second, that these good tidings consist of the reward for the deeds of the believers, which is that "no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve." This is only through their witnessing this reward in the abode where they reside; for their state is one of witnessing (المشاهدة) rather than inference (الاستدلال). Thus, the verse contains an indication of the survival of the human being after death in the period between death and the Day of Resurrection. We have detailed the discussion on this in the discourse on the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — under His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ** — **"Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead’"** (2:154).

His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ** — **"They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace..."** (3:171). This being glad (الاستبشار) is more general than being glad for the state of others or for their own state. The proof for this is His saying: **وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful"** (3:171), for in its absolute sense, it encompasses everyone. Perhaps this is the subtle point in the repetition of "being glad" (الاستبشار) as well as the repetition of "grace" (الفضل); so reflect upon the verse.

"Grace" (الفضل) and "blessing" (النعمة) are mentioned in the indefinite form, and "provision" (الرزق) is left vague in the verses so that the mind of the listener may go in every possible direction. Similarly, "fear" (الخوف) and "grief" (الحزن) are left vague to indicate generality within the context of negation.

Reflection on the verses reveals that they are concerned with: first, explaining the reward of the believers; second, that this reward is their provision (رزقهم) with Allah the Glorified; third, that this provision is a blessing (نعمة) from Allah and a grace (فضل); and fourth, that what characterizes this blessing and grace is that "no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve."

This sentence—namely His saying: **أن لا خوف عليهم ولا هم يحزنون** — **"no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve"**—is a wondrous statement. The more one reflects upon it, the more its meaning expands in subtlety, delicacy, and ease of expression. The first thing that appears from its meaning is that fear and grief are removed from them. Fear (الخوف) only arises from a possible, anticipated matter that would cause the loss of some aspect of human happiness that one considers oneself to possess. Similarly, grief (الحزن) only arises from an occurred matter that causes such a loss. Thus, a calamity or any dreaded thing is feared as long as it has not yet occurred; once it occurs, fear vanishes and grief sets in. Therefore, there is no fear after the occurrence, and no grief before it.

The removal of absolute fear from a person only happens when the various blessings (النعم) they possess are not subject to cessation. The removal of absolute grief is only possible for them if they do not lose any type of their happiness, neither initially nor after possessing it. Thus, Allah's removal of absolute fear and grief from a person means that He pours upon them everything they can possibly enjoy and find pleasure in, and that this shall not be subject to cessation. This is the eternity of happiness (خلود السعادة) for the human being and their eternity within it.

From here, it becomes clear that the negation of fear and grief is the very same thing as the human being "receiving provision with Allah." For He, the Glorified, says: **وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ** — **"and what is with Allah is better"** (3:198), and He says: **وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** — **"and what is with Allah is enduring"** (16:96). These two verses indicate that what is with Allah is an enduring blessing (نعمة باقية) that is not mixed with any affliction, nor subject to any annihilation.

It also becomes clear that their negation is the very same thing as the affirmation of blessing (النعمة) and grace (الفضل), which is the gift (العطية). However, it was mentioned at the beginning of the book, and will follow in His saying, the Exalted: **مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — **"with those whom Allah has blessed"** (4:69), that when "blessing" (النعمة) is used in the parlance of the Quran, it refers to the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). Accordingly, the meaning is: Allah takes charge of their affairs and singles them out with a gift from Him.

As for the possibility that "grace" (الفضل) refers to a bestowal exceeding what they deserve by their deeds, while "blessing" (النعمة) refers to what corresponds to their deeds—this is not harmonized with His saying: **وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful"** (3:171), for "reward" (الأجر) implies desert. You have already seen that these phrases—namely His saying: **عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ** — **"receiving provision from their Lord"** (3:169), His saying: **فَرِحِينَ بِما** — **"rejoicing in what..."** (3:170), His saying: **يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ** — **"They rejoice in Allah’s blessing..."** (3:171), and His saying: **وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful"** (3:171)—all return to a single reality.

There are other discussions regarding these verses, some of which preceded in the commentary on His saying: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ** — **"Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead’"** (2:154). Perhaps Allah will grant us success to exhaust what we can of the discussion regarding them in the appropriate places to come, if Allah the Exalted wills.
